primary_diagnosis.name: Acute irritant and allergic contact dermatitis of the scalp, superimposed on androgenetic alopecia

differential.diagnosis: Acute irritant/allergic contact dermatitis of the scalp
differential.evidence_basis: Severe burning, weeping and micro-vesiculation confined to the exact area a novel topical was applied within an hour — the classic pattern of an acute contact reaction.
An unregulated compound of unknown concentration can cause a caustic injury; depth cannot be graded over a video call and must be considered.
A novel chemical exposure warrants an active screen for airway/systemic features before it can be excluded.

differential.diagnosis: Cellulitis / secondary bacterial infection of the scalp
differential.evidence_basis: Weeping, denuded skin can become secondarily infected; screen for fever, spreading erythema and purulence.
Far too acute, painful, weeping and vesicular, and precisely time-locked to product application — not the greasy scaling of seborrhoeic dermatitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the leading diagnosis is a product-triggered acute contact dermatitis, but chemical burn depth and a systemic allergic reaction are the important-not-to-miss considerations that drive the telehealth screen and disposition.

differential.diagnosis: Acute irritant/allergic contact dermatitis of the scalp
differential.evidence_basis: No airway/systemic features and no signs of spreading infection — the picture is a localised acute contact reaction to the applied product.

differential.diagnosis: Chemical burn of the scalp (superficial vs deeper)
differential.evidence_basis: Even with intact-appearing skin on video, the true depth of a caustic injury from an unknown compound cannot be excluded remotely — the residual reason for urgent in-person assessment.
No lip/tongue/throat swelling, no voice change and no breathing difficulty on direct questioning.
Absent now (afebrile, no spreading erythema, no purulence) but a foreseeable complication of the weeping barrier breach — a safety-net target, not a current diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen removes anaphylaxis but cannot remove the depth-of-injury uncertainty; that uncertainty, not the diagnosis, is what fixes the urgent in-person disposition.

differential.diagnosis: Acute irritant and allergic contact dermatitis of the scalp, superimposed on androgenetic alopecia
differential.evidence_basis: Product-triggered acute contact dermatitis with chemical-injury features; systemically well; the chronic patterned hair loss is an incidental background, not the acute problem.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-point is recognition of the acute product-induced skin injury (not the chronic hair loss), immediate decontamination, an airway/anaphylaxis screen, and urgent same-day in-person assessment to grade the injury.

red_flags.description: Swelling of the lips, tongue, face or throat, voice change or breathing difficulty — a systemic allergic reaction / anaphylaxis after a novel chemical exposure.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would signify a life-threatening systemic allergic reaction requiring immediate adrenaline and 000; must be actively screened after any new chemical exposure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A developing airway reaction could be missed and progress to obstruction.

red_flags.description: Severe painful weeping injury from an unregulated topical of unknown concentration — chemical-burn depth cannot be assessed remotely.
red_flags.clinical_significance: A caustic chemical injury may be deeper than it appears; depth determines whether specialist wound care or debridement is needed and cannot be graded over video.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An under-appreciated deep chemical burn could cause permanent scarring alopecia and delayed healing.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection of the weeping skin — spreading erythema beyond the treated area, fever/rigors or thick foul-smelling pus.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Denuded, weeping skin is a portal for infection; cellulitis of the scalp/face can progress and needs systemic antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated spreading infection could progress to systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Extensive weeping/denuded surface over the whole vertex and frontal scalp — significant barrier breach.
red_flags.clinical_significance: A large raw, weeping area carries pain, fluid loss, infection and scarring risk and reinforces the need for in-person wound assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-treatment risks secondary infection and permanent scarring alopecia.

pathophysiology_summary: Application of an unregulated, high-concentration topical compound to the scalp produced an acute contact dermatitis with both an irritant (direct cytotoxic/caustic) component and, plausibly, a superimposed type-IV (delayed) allergic hypersensitivity. Disruption of the epidermal barrier and mediator release cause the sharply demarcated erythema, oedema, intense pain, serous weeping and micro-vesiculation seen precisely at the sites of application. Because the compound's identity and concentration are unknown, the injury sits on a spectrum from a self-limiting contact dermatitis to a caustic chemical burn — a distinction that depends on injury depth and cannot be resolved over telehealth. The chronic androgenetic alopecia underneath (miniaturised follicles, patterned recession) is an unrelated, benign background; the acute, painful, exudative process is the presenting problem, and healing of a deep injury could leave scarring (cicatricial) alopecia in place of the benign pattern.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the chronic, benign patterned hair loss named in the referral/complaint and dismissing the presentation as 'just a hair problem' — missing the acute chemical/allergic skin injury that is the real emergency-adjacent issue.
Treating a product-induced weeping scalp as a trivial rash and forgetting that telehealth cannot grade the depth of a chemical injury from an unknown compound — leading to under-triage.
Failing to actively screen for airway/systemic features after a novel chemical exposure before reassuring.

Sending a systemically well patient with no airway features straight to 000/ED — over-triage that ignores that immediate decontamination plus urgent same-day in-person assessment is the proportionate response.
Prescribing empiric oral antibiotics for weeping skin with no fever, no spreading erythema and no purulence — treating exudate as infection.

cannot_determine_via_chat: The true depth of the chemical injury / whether the epidermis is intact versus full-thickness
Exact blood pressure, heart rate and temperature without a home device (source in-person values BP 138/86, HR 95, Temp 37.1, RR 16, SpO2 98%)
The precise extent and margins of the erythema and any early secondary infection
The identity of the offending chemical and any responsible contact allergen (requires later patch testing)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise an acute product-induced scalp injury; instruct immediate patient-performed decontamination (rinse the scalp with copious cool/lukewarm water, remove all residual product, stop using it); actively screen for and exclude airway/systemic allergic features; arrange URGENT same-day in-person assessment (GP, urgent care, or ED if none available) to grade the injury and start treatment; safety-net to 000 for any lip/tongue/throat swelling or breathing difficulty.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the trigger and pattern make contact dermatitis clear, but the depth/severity and secondary-infection risk cannot be graded remotely. The dominant risk is UNDER-action (routine reassurance) rather than mis-diagnosis; a secondary risk is reflexive over-escalation to 000 when no systemic features are present.
